\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Circuit Compression}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.LMN.CircuitCompression
\section{Overview}

This file develops Steps 6 and 7 of the LMN argument: once every layer-2 gate of a
depth-$d$ circuit can be switched to a width-$\ell$ CNF, two adjacent layers of the same
gate type can be merged, producing a depth-$(d-1)$ circuit of width at most $\ell$. The
module supplies the algebraic flattening identities (concatenation of CNF/DNF clause
lists preserves width and computes the conjunction/disjunction of the parts), the
resulting one-layer compression theorems, and the probabilistic one-step reduction
bounds obtained from the switching-lemma union bound under a Bernoulli random
restriction.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Declarations}

\begin{definition}[Concatenation of a list of lists]
\lean{LMN.listConcat}
\leanok
Flattens a list of lists $[\,\ell_1, \ell_2, \dots\,]$ into the single list
$\ell_1 \mathbin{+\!\!+} \ell_2 \mathbin{+\!\!+} \cdots$, defined by recursion on the
outer list. Used to merge the clause (or term) lists of several CNFs (or DNFs).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Bounded width iff all terms bounded]
\lean{LMN.width_le_iff_forall}
\leanok
\uses{Term, Term.width}
For a list of terms $ts$, the folded maximum of their widths is at most $\ell$ if and
only if every term $t \in ts$ satisfies $t.\mathrm{width} \le \ell$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[CNF concatenation preserves width]
\lean{LMN.cnf_concat_width_le}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.width, LMN.listConcat}
If CNFs $\psi_1, \dots, \psi_s$ each have width at most $\ell$, then the CNF obtained by
concatenating all their clause lists also has width at most $\ell$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[CNF concatenation evaluates as conjunction]
\lean{LMN.cnf_concat_eval}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, LMN.listConcat}
For any input $x$, the value of the concatenated CNF at $x$ equals the conjunction over
all $\psi$ in the list of $\psi.\mathrm{eval}\,x$; that is, it holds iff every component
CNF holds at $x$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[DNF concatenation preserves width]
\lean{LMN.dnf_concat_width_le}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.width, DNF, DNF.width, LMN.cnf_concat_width_le, LMN.listConcat, Term.width, cnfToDualDNF_width}
If DNFs $\varphi_1, \dots, \varphi_s$ each have width at most $\ell$, then the DNF
obtained by concatenating all their term lists also has width at most $\ell$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[DNF concatenation evaluates as disjunction]
\lean{LMN.dnf_concat_eval}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, LMN.listConcat}
For any input $x$, the value of the concatenated DNF at $x$ equals the disjunction over
all $\varphi$ in the list of $\varphi.\mathrm{eval}\,x$; that is, it holds iff some
component DNF holds at $x$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Circuit compression for CNFs under AND]
\lean{LMN.compression_and_of_cnfs}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.width, LMN.cnf_concat_eval, LMN.cnf_concat_width_le, LMN.listConcat}
Let an AND gate have a list of children, each of which computes a function expressible
by a CNF of width at most $\ell$. Then the AND of all children is itself computed by a
single CNF of width at most $\ell$, namely the concatenation of the children's clause
lists. This collapses the AND layer and the CNF layer into one.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Circuit compression for DNFs under OR]
\lean{LMN.compression_or_of_dnfs}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, LMN.dnf_concat_eval, LMN.dnf_concat_width_le, LMN.listConcat}
Dual to the AND case: if an OR gate has children each expressible by a DNF of width at
most $\ell$, then the OR of all children is computed by a single DNF of width at most
$\ell$, obtained by concatenating the children's term lists.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[One-step reduction failure bound]
\lean{LMN.one_step_reduction_failure_bound}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.width, DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, LMN.layer2_cnf_replaceability_union_bound, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn}
For $s_2$ layer-2 DNF gates of width at most $w$ (with the usual no-duplicate-variable
and nodup hypotheses on terms) and a Bernoulli restriction with parameter
$p \le 1/(40w)$, the probability that some gate fails to admit a width-$\ell$ CNF
representation of its restriction is at most
$s_2 \cdot \left( (1/2)^{\ell} + \exp(-np/3) \right)$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[One-step decision-tree depth bound]
\lean{LMN.one_step_dtDepth_bound}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, LMN.switching_bernoulli_union_bound, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight_sum_one, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn, dtDepth}
Under a Bernoulli restriction with $p \le 1/(40w)$ on $s_2$ width-$w$ DNF gates, the
probability that every restricted gate has decision-tree depth at most $\ell$ is at
least $1 - s_2 \cdot \left( (1/2)^{\ell} + \exp(-np/3) \right)$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{lemma}[Complement probability sums to one]
\lean{LMN.bernoulliRestrProb_complement}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight_sum_one}
For a Bernoulli restriction probability with parameter $0 \le p \le 1$ and a decidable
predicate $A$ on restrictions, $\Pr[A] + \Pr[\lnot A] = 1$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[One-step reduction with compression]
\lean{LMN.one_step_reduction_with_compression}
\leanok
\uses{CNF, CNF.eval, CNF.width, DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, LMN.one_step_reduction_failure_bound, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.restrictFn}
Combining Steps 6 and 7: after a Bernoulli restriction with $p \le 1/(40w)$ on $s_2$
width-$w$ DNF gates, the probability that every restricted gate admits a width-$\ell$
CNF representation is at least $1 - s_2 \cdot \left( (1/2)^{\ell} + \exp(-np/3) \right)$,
so that the compression of Step 6 applies and the circuit reduces to depth $(d-1)$ with
width at most $\ell$.
\end{theorem}
